canto_title: Recognition of Souls and the Warning of the Tree

scenes.summary: Dante describes the steady pace of the travelers and the admiring gazes of the souls passing them.
Dante asks the soul he is walking with for news regarding Piccarda Donati.
The soul reveals that Piccarda is in Heaven and begins identifying other souls by name.
The soul continues naming various figures, including Ubaldo da Pesaro and Boniface.
Dante notices a soul muttering and asks him to speak clearly so they may understand one another.
The soul introduces himself and asks if Dante recognizes the author of the 'nine rhymes'.
Dante identifies himself as a poet, prompting the soul to explain the literary connection that binds them together.
A poetic simile compares the movement of the crowd to a flock of geese flying in formation.
Forese Giunchi leaves the group, walking ahead of Dante.
Dante speaks with Forese about the difficulty of returning to the world of the living.
Forese warns Dante of the approaching Beast and the destruction it brings.
Forese departs the group with the swiftness and dignity of a knight in a cavalry charge.
Dante observes souls beneath a tree, reaching upward in a state of desperate, unfulfilled desire.
A voice warns the travelers not to approach the tree, which is linked to the Fall of Man.
A voice recalls stories of Theseus and the Hebrew warriors to illustrate the nature of the souls.
The group passes through dense thickets, observing the consequences of gluttony.
A sudden voice startles Dante, causing him to recoil like a frightened animal.
Dante sees a glowing, intense figure who warns that they must turn back if they wish to ascend.
Overwhelmed by the sight, Dante turns back to his guides, Virgil and Statius.
A fragrant, pleasant wind like that of May passes over Dante, bringing the scent of blossoms.
Within the breeze, Dante hears a beatific blessing regarding the virtue of moderation.
